title: Effect of cations on van der Waals interactions between particles in aqueous alkali nitrate electrolytes
authors: Micah P. Prange, Jaehun Chun, Gregory K. Schenter, Elias Nakouzi, Yihui Wei, Aurora E. Clark, Kevin M. Rosso, Carolyn I. Pearce
abstract: The van der Waals interaction has been extensively studied for colloidal forces and resultant emergent phenomena such as colloidal stability, aggregation, and suspension rheology, but the effect of electrolytes on this interaction, especially at intermediate and high electrolyte concentrations, remains incompletely understood. We have extended the Lifshitz theory for van der Waals interactions in pure water to alkali nitrate solutions at arbitrary concentrations by developing a dielectric response model for alkali nitrate solutions that is based on electronic structure calculations of the molecular constituents. Due to their importance in catalysis, ceramics, and coating technologies, the Hamaker constants for rutile, boehmite, and alumina nanoparticles suspended in alkali nitrate solutions are calculated as a function of salt concentration. Contrary to prevailing assumptions, increasing the concentration of sodium (Na), potassium (K), and rubidium (Rb) nitrate solutions causes appreciable increases of the Hamaker constants relative to pure water instead of decreases, whereas cesium nitrate (CsNO3) has almost no effect on the Hamaker constant. We discussed the influence of the solution molar volume, the polarizability of the dissolved ions, and optical properties of the interacting particles in the context of previously published work. Our study indicates a non-vanishing role of van der Waals interactions on colloidal stability at intermediate and high electrolyte concentrations, leading to physical insights on emergent phenomena associated with nanoparticles.
summary: This theoretical study calculates how van der Waals interactions between nanoparticles are modified by dissolved alkali nitrate electrolytes using an extended Lifshitz theory. By employing advanced electronic structure calculations, the authors find that increasing salt concentration can actually increase the Hamaker constant for certain ions, contradicting older assumptions and highlighting the persistent role of these forces in colloidal stability.
main_problem: The study aims to resolve the incomplete understanding of how electrolytes affect van der Waals interactions between particles, especially at high concentrations.
main_result: Contrary to prior assumptions, increasing concentrations of $	ext{NaNO}_3$, $	ext{KNO}_3$, and $	ext{RbNO}_3$ cause appreciable increases in the Hamaker constants, suggesting van der Waals interactions remain significant for colloidal stability.
method: The authors extended Lifshitz theory by developing a dielectric response model for alkali nitrate solutions, calculated using electronic structure methods like LR-TDDFT.
model_or_system: The system involves nanoparticles (rutile, boehmite, alumina) suspended in aqueous alkali nitrate electrolytes ($	ext{MNO}_3$). The theory models the interaction potential via the Hamaker constant, which depends on the dielectric response of the solvent and ions.
key_observables: Hamaker constants ($A$), dielectric functions ($\epsilon(\omega)$ and $\epsilon(i\xi)$), and the concentration dependence of these constants.
important_parameters: Intermediate and high electrolyte concentrations, the cation identity ($	ext{Na}^+, 	ext{K}^+, 	ext{Rb}^+, 	ext{Cs}^+$), and the temperature ($T=300	ext{K}$).
main_assumptions: The model neglects Debye-type relaxation ($\hbar_7 = 0$) and assumes the dielectric response can be modeled via linear combinations of molecular/ionic oscillator strengths.
figures_summary: Figures compare dielectric functions using TDDFT vs. pole models, plot the Hamaker constant vs. cation species and concentration, and show photoabsorption cross-sections for various species.
paper_structure: The paper builds from a literature review of interparticle forces and solution chemistry, develops a theoretical framework based on extending Lifshitz theory using TDDFT to calculate the dielectric response, and presents calculations showing the concentration dependence of the Hamaker constant.
why_interesting: While focused on classical colloidal forces, the rigorous application of advanced quantum mechanical methods (TDDFT) to model the dielectric response of complex ionic solutions provides a sophisticated theoretical framework for understanding many-body interactions in condensed matter systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
